test_gaps.title: Single dead-letter batch larger than the cap must be written anyway (F03 livelock prevention), not Blocked
test_gaps.target: crates/weir-server/src/drain/mod.rs::commit_batch (permanent-error branch, lines 978-991; and the partial-dead-letter branch, lines 899-914) — the `if estimated > config.dead_letter_max_bytes` overshoot arm
test_gaps.property: When a single permanently-rejected batch's estimated sealed size ALONE exceeds dead_letter_max_bytes, commit_batch must write it to the dead-letter dir and return BatchResult::Ok (overshoot once) — NOT BatchResult::Blocked. Blocking on a batch that can never fit (even an empty dir won't hold it) would wedge the drain in a permanent block->retry livelock. The cap bounds steady-state growth, not one oversized permanent rejection.
test_gaps.test_design: SETUP: open a DeadLetterWriter on a temp dir (total_bytes starts at 0). Build a config with a TINY dead_letter_max_bytes (e.g. 10 bytes) via tight_dl_config so any real batch's estimated_segment_bytes exceeds it (header+footer+sentinel framing alone is ~60 bytes). Build a MockSink that returns Err(MockError::Permanent) for the batch. Use payloads = vec![Payload::from(b"a-record")]. ASSERTIONS: (1) block_on(commit_batch(&payloads, &sink, &config, &metrics, &mut dl)) returns BatchResult::Ok (matches!(result, BatchResult::Ok)) — proving the overshoot arm fired, NOT Blocked. (2) records_dead_lettered(&metrics) == 1 — the oversized batch was actually written. (3) dl.total_bytes() > config.dead_letter_max_bytes — the dir is now over-cap (overshoot happened). (4) Exactly one dl_*.wab.sealed file exists in dir/dead_letter. Mirror the same for the partial-dead-letter branch (lines 899-914) with MockSink::ok_with_dead_letter where the dead-lettered sub-vector alone exceeds the cap — assert BatchResult::Ok again. This pins F03: the only existing cap tests all use a batch that FITS but a pre-filled dir, so they only ever exercise the `return BatchResult::Blocked` arm; the `estimated > cap` overshoot arm has zero coverage.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: I read every cap-related test in drain/mod.rs (blocked_when_permanent_error_and_dead_letter_cap_exceeded L1989, blocked_does_not_call_commit_while_waiting L2033, blocked_unblocks_and_retries_same_segment L2077, dead_letter_full_total_increments_once_per_entry_not_per_wake L2130, blocked_duration_set_on_entry_and_cleared_on_exit L2169, shutdown_while_blocked_does_not_confirm_segment L2621) and drain/dead_letter.rs (would_exceed_cap_boundary L258). ALL of them pre-fill the dead-letter dir with a 200-byte file under a 100-byte cap and send a tiny batch, so they only ever drive the `else { return BatchResult::Blocked }` arm. None drives `if estimated > config.dead_letter_max_bytes` (the F03 overshoot). The night-sweep findings.json (S01-S46) contains no F03/overshoot test. The branch at mod.rs:979-990 (and the twin at 900-913) — which prevents a permanent block->retry livelock — has zero direct or indirect coverage.
test_gaps.verify_reason: Verified: the `estimated > cap` overshoot arms at mod.rs:979-990 and 900-913 (F03 livelock prevention) have zero coverage — all existing cap tests pre-fill a 200-byte dir under a 100-byte cap with a tiny batch, driving only the `else { return Blocked }` arm; the test reuses real helpers (tight_dl_config, ok_with_dead_letter, block_on direct-call pattern), is fully deterministic (no threads/timing), and pins a real load-bearing property (write-anyway vs permanent block↔retry livelock).

test_gaps.title: replay_unconfirmed must SKIP an already-confirmed segment (dedup guard against re-delivering drained backlog every restart)
test_gaps.property: A sealed segment that already has a valid `.confirmed` sidecar (i.e. it was delivered to the sink and confirmed on a prior run) is NOT re-sent to the drain channel by replay_unconfirmed, while a sibling sealed segment WITHOUT a sidecar IS sent. This is the dedup boundary: without it, every restart re-queues the entire confirmed-but-not-yet-deleted backlog (a crash between .confirmed-write and segment-delete leaves the sealed file on disk), re-delivering all of it to the sink — turning a single bounded duplicate into an unbounded re-processing storm that grows with WAB size.
test_gaps.test_design: In the existing wab::mod tests module (which already has `replay_drains_shard_dirs_beyond_configured_count` and `tmp_dir`/`shard_dir_path`/`segment_path` helpers and imports `WabSegment`): create one shard dir shard_00. Seal TWO segments: seg_00000001 (write a record, seal) and seg_00000002 (write a record, seal). For seg_00000001 ONLY, write a valid `.confirmed` sidecar next to it — reuse `crate::wab::format::confirmed_path_for(&sealed1)` for the path and `crate::wab::format::build_confirmed(0, 1, 1)` for valid bytes (this is exactly how recovery.rs's `check_confirmed_valid_returns_true` constructs a valid sidecar, so parse_confirmed will accept it and check_confirmed returns Ok(true)). Build a bounded(256) channel + Metrics, call `replay_unconfirmed(&dir, 1, &tx, &metrics)`, drop(tx), collect `rx.iter()`. Assert: exactly ONE path is sent (the confirmed seg_00000001 is skipped, the unconfirmed seg_00000002 is queued); the received path ends with `seg_00000002.wab.sealed`; and neither the still-present sealed1 nor its sidecar were touched/deleted by replay (replay is read-only on confirmed segments — cleanup is a separate concern). Optionally also assert `metrics.recovery_records_replayed.get() == 1` (only the unconfirmed segment's footer count is counted). Note vs existing coverage: recovery.rs unit-tests `check_confirmed` in isolation (valid/missing/bad-crc), and wab::mod's two replay tests only exercise the `Ok(false)` queue-for-replay branch — none wire a confirmed sidecar THROUGH replay_unconfirmed to prove the skip decision, which is the actual dedup-on-restart guard.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: Checked all replay coverage. wab/mod.rs has exactly two replay_unconfirmed tests: `replay_needs_a_live_consumer_for_backlog_over_channel_capacity` (B3, all 300 segments unconfirmed) and `replay_drains_shard_dirs_beyond_configured_count` (S05, all 4 segments unconfirmed) — both exercise ONLY the Ok(false) queue branch; neither places a .confirmed sidecar. The Ok(true) skip branch (mod.rs:370-372) is never reached by any replay test. recovery.rs unit-tests `check_confirmed` directly (check_confirmed_valid_returns_true / _missing_file_returns_false / _bad_crc_quarantines / _unknown_version) but never through replay_unconfirmed, so the load-bearing decision 'a valid sidecar ⇒ do not re-queue this segment to the drain' is unproven. The system-level crash tests (wab_data_preserved_across_crash_restart, recovery_replays_records_after_crash) assert recovery_records_replayed_total advances but never restart a server whose segments were ALREADY confirmed, so they cannot catch a regression where confirmed segments get re-replayed. DST faults only at segment-creation time and never touches replay/check_confirmed at all. A regression dropping or inverting this check ships as silent unbounded duplicate re-delivery on every restart.
test_gaps.verify_reason: The Ok(true) skip branch (mod.rs:370-372) is reachable but never exercised through replay_unconfirmed by any test (both existing replay tests seal segments with no .confirmed sidecar, hitting only Ok(false); recovery.rs tests check_confirmed in isolation), so the load-bearing dedup-on-restart property (confirmed segment NOT re-queued while unconfirmed sibling IS) is genuinely unproven; the draft uses real pub helpers/metrics, reaches the branch via a CRC-valid build_confirmed(0,1,1) sidecar, and is deterministic (synchronous replay + drained channel, single unambiguous queued path).

test_gaps.title: Recovery's mid-payload truncation branch is never exercised — the existing crash test only hits the mid-CRC branch
test_gaps.property: When a segment is torn off partway through a record's PAYLOAD bytes (length and CRC fields fully present, payload short), recovery truncates at the last fully-valid record boundary, seals, and recovers exactly the records that preceded the torn one — with no spurious extra record and no error.
test_gaps.test_design: Setup: create a clean segment with 3 records (b"record1", b"record2", b"record3", each 7 bytes → 15 on-disk each over a 24-byte header). Drop the WabSegment so bytes flush. Then truncate to leave header(24) + rec1(15) + rec2(15) + rec3's len(4) + rec3's crc(4) + 3 of rec3's 7 payload bytes = 24+15+15+4+4+3 = 65 bytes. This is the DISTINCT mid-payload branch: read_exact of len_buf succeeds, read_exact of crc_buf succeeds, read_exact of the payload returns UnexpectedEof at recovery.rs:280. Run recover_segment. Assertions: returns Ok; SegmentReader::open(sealed) yields exactly 2 records equal to b"record1" and b"record2"; the active path no longer exists; the sealed path ends with .wab.sealed. Contrast with the existing recovery_crash_simulation_truncate_mid_record, whose truncate_at = 24+15+15+4 stops with the length field complete and zero CRC bytes read — that lands on the mid-CRC branch (line 269), not mid-payload.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: recovery.rs has recovery_crash_simulation_truncate_mid_record (truncate_at = 24+15+15+4, which per its own comment 'stops mid-CRC of record3' — i.e. the line-269 mid-CRC-field branch), recovery_truncates_at_crc_mismatch_keeping_valid_prefix (line-288 CRC-mismatch branch), recovery_truncates_at_oversized_payload_len (line-255 oversized branch), and recovery_stops_at_partial_seal_sentinel (line-250 sentinel branch). The mid-PAYLOAD branch at recovery.rs:278-285 (full len+crc read, short payload read) has no test that lands on it: every existing truncation test stops before or within the 8-byte len+crc prefix, never inside the payload region. A regression that mis-handled a short payload read (e.g. treating it as a valid zero-length record, or not breaking) would not be caught.
test_gaps.verify_reason: Verified against recovery.rs: the mid-payload UnexpectedEof branch (lines 278-285) is genuinely uncovered — existing truncation tests hit mid-CRC-field (269), CRC-mismatch (288), oversized-len (255), and sentinel (250), but none reads a full 8-byte len+crc prefix then a short payload; offset math (24+15+15+4+4+3=65) is correct given the 24-byte header and len(4)+crc(4)+payload on-disk layout, and the test is deterministic (set_len-based, no timing).

test_gaps.title: recover_open_segments must isolate a poisoned segment: one un-recoverable file in a shard dir must not abort recovery of its healthy siblings
test_gaps.target: crates/weir-server/src/wab/recovery.rs::recover_shard_dir (per-segment match arm, lines 119-127) reached via recover_open_segments
test_gaps.property: Within a single shard directory holding multiple unsealed segments, a segment whose recovery FAILS (e.g. bad magic → quarantine → Err) is logged and skipped, while every other healthy unsealed segment in the same directory is still sealed; recover_open_segments returns Ok(()) overall (a quarantined corrupt segment does not take the whole daemon's recovery offline).
test_gaps.test_design: Setup: tmp wab_dir with one shard dir shard_00 containing TWO unsealed segments at distinct counters: seg_00000001.wab written via WabSegment with a valid record b"healthy-1", then corrupted in its magic bytes (overwrite first 4 bytes with b"XXXX") to force recover_segment to quarantine+Err; and seg_00000002.wab written via WabSegment with b"healthy-2" left active. Call recover_open_segments(&wab_dir, &metrics). Assertions: (1) the whole call returns Ok(()) — the per-segment Err is swallowed by the match arm at recovery.rs:123-126, not propagated; (2) seg_00000002.wab.sealed exists and SegmentReader reads back b"healthy-2" — the healthy sibling was sealed despite the corrupt one; (3) seg_00000001.wab is gone from the shard dir (quarantined) and wab_dir/quarantine/ contains shard_00__seg_00000001.wab; (4) metrics.recovery_segments_quarantined.get() == 1. This pins the fault-isolation contract: a single corrupt file can't deny recovery to the rest of a shard. Note: because active.sort() puts the corrupt counter-1 file first, this also proves the early failure does not short-circuit the loop (order-independence).
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: recover_open_segments_seals_every_segment_per_shard_deterministically covers TWO HEALTHY segments in one shard (both seal, deterministic order). recover_open_segments_skips_dead_letter_dir covers the reserved-dir skip. recovery_quarantines_bad_magic exercises recover_SEGMENT (the single-file fn) directly and asserts the Err — but it never drives recover_open_segments/recover_shard_dir, so the per-segment error-swallowing match arm at recovery.rs:123-126 (the `Err(e) => error!(...)` that logs-and-continues and lets the function return Ok) is never proven to (a) keep going to seal the next segment and (b) not propagate the Err out of recover_open_segments. No existing test mixes a recover-failing segment with a healthy one in the same shard dir, so the fault-isolation guarantee — one bad file can't deny recovery to the rest of the shard — is unproven.
test_gaps.verify_reason: Verified: no existing test drives recover_open_segments/recover_shard_dir with a recover-failing segment beside a healthy sibling, so the log-and-continue match arm (recovery.rs:123-126) that swallows the per-segment Err and still returns Ok(()) is unproven; the test is meaningful (fault-isolation durability contract) and deterministic (active.sort() forces corrupt-first ordering, write_vectored persists bytes before the magic overwrite, recover_segment opens its own handles, no timing).

test_gaps.title: TLS handshake-timeout slowloris guard drops a stalled TCP client and bumps the timeout metric
test_gaps.target: tcp::run handshake select! — the `Err(_elapsed)` arm (tcp.rs:206-218) that increments tls_handshake_failures{reason=timeout} and drops the connection
test_gaps.property: A client that completes the TCP three-way handshake but then sends nothing (or stalls mid-TLS-handshake) is dropped within ~handshake_timeout_secs rather than holding its shared semaphore permit + JoinSet slot indefinitely, and the tls_handshake_failures{reason=timeout} counter increments. This is the TLS-layer analogue of the read-timeout slowloris guard the connection layer already proves.
test_gaps.test_design: RISK RATIONALE (why untested-ness is dangerous): the handshake timeout is a DoS defense; without it a flood of clients that complete the TCP accept but never send a ClientHello pin permits from the SHARED max_connections semaphore (also used by the Unix listener) and starve all legitimate producers. The F29 comment at tcp.rs:183 explicitly cites this threat, yet no test proves the timeout branch fires, drops the connection, or records the metric. Setup (in tls_listener.rs, which already has the TlsFixture + weir_server harness): start the daemon with a SHORT handshake_timeout (e.g. 1s; expose via the harness or use the default and widen the time bound). Open a RAW std::net::TcpStream to srv.tcp_addr() and send NOTHING (no ClientHello) — modelling a peer that finished the TCP accept but stalls the TLS handshake. Assertions: (1) the daemon does not hang — a second, well-formed mTLS client (valid CA-signed cert) connecting after still gets its push acked, proving the stalled connection did not exhaust the permit pool or wedge the accept loop; (2) the stalled raw socket is dropped by the server within roughly handshake_timeout (read() on the raw stream returns Ok(0)/EOF shortly after the timeout); (3) if the metrics endpoint is scraped, weir_tls_handshake_failures with reason="timeout" is >= 1. The classify_handshake_error unit tests cover no_client_cert/bad_cert/other but NOT the timeout reason, which is produced by a SEPARATE code path (the Err(_elapsed) arm), so this is the only place the timeout branch and its metric label are exercised.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: tcp.rs::tests contains only classify_handshake_error unit tests for reasons no_client_cert/bad_cert(+variants)/other — none for the `timeout` reason, which is emitted by the separate Err(_elapsed) arm (tcp.rs:206-218), not by classify_handshake_error. tls_listener.rs integration tests cover valid/no-cert/wrong-ca/expired handshakes (all of which complete a TLS handshake) but never a client that stalls mid-handshake, so the handshake_timeout branch and its tls_handshake_failures{reason=timeout} metric are exercised nowhere. The night sweep (S01-S34) added no handshake-timeout test.
test_gaps.verify_reason: The Err(_elapsed) handshake-timeout arm (tcp.rs:206-218) emitting tls_handshake_failures{reason=timeout} is a distinct code path from classify_handshake_error; unit tests cover only no_client_cert/bad_cert/other, and all tls_listener.rs clients complete a handshake — so the timeout branch and its metric label are genuinely uncovered, the property is load-bearing (shared max_connections DoS guard, F29), and it is deterministic if tls_handshake_timeout_secs is shortened to ~1s (which the proposal itself flags and the harness supports via extra_config, against the 5s read deadline).

test_gaps.title: read_body_capped never tested with a body larger than the cap (the mid-chunk break is dead to tests)
test_gaps.target: crates/weir-server/src/sink/mod.rs::read_body_capped — the cap branch `if take < chunk.len() { break }` at mod.rs:100-101
test_gaps.property: read_body_capped returns at most `cap` bytes regardless of how many bytes the response carries, and stops reading once the cap is reached (S28 memory bound: peak buffering is bounded at concurrency × cap even against a downstream that streams an unbounded body).
test_gaps.test_design:
read_body_capped takes a reqwest::Response, which can't be hand-constructed easily, so drive it through a real loopback request inside the sink/mod.rs test module: spawn a tiny tokio TcpListener that, on accept, reads/discards the request header block then writes a response whose body is much larger than a small test cap (e.g. respond `HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Length: 1000

` followed by 1000 bytes). In the test, build a `reqwest::Client`, GET the URL, and call `read_body_capped(resp, 100)`. Assertions: (1) the returned Vec has length exactly 100 (cap), proving the mid-chunk break fired and the function did not buffer all 1000 bytes; (2) a second call with `read_body_capped(resp, 4096)` against the same 1000-byte body returns exactly 1000 (the `Ok(None)` end-of-body branch — cap not reached). This pins both the cap-hit and the natural-EOF exits. Keep the cap small so the assertion is exact regardless of reqwest's internal chunk sizes. Gate the test the same way the fn is gated: `#[cfg(any(feature = "http-sink", feature = "clickhouse-sink"))]`.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: sink/mod.rs has a #[cfg(test)] mod tests (mod.rs:111-158) but it only tests redact_url_password and sanitize_log_excerpt — there is NO test for read_body_capped at all. Grepped all sink files for RESPONSE_BODY_CAP / 65536 / `cap`: the constant is referenced only at the two call sites (http.rs:171/222, clickhouse.rs:382) and no test feeds a body exceeding the cap or asserts a bounded return. The HTTP/CH commit tests use empty or tiny bodies, so the mid-chunk break (mod.rs:100) and the cap-reached loop exit (mod.rs:95) are never reached. This is the S28 memory-bound guard the docs lean on; it is currently unproven.
test_gaps.verify_reason: read_body_capped has zero tests; all existing sink tests use bodies <=12 bytes, so the cap-hit break (mod.rs:100) and loop guard (mod.rs:95) are never exercised — the proposed loopback test deterministically pins both the cap-hit and natural-EOF exits without depending on reqwest chunk sizing.

test_gaps.title: F58 empty/whitespace log_level must fall back to "info", not silently disable all logging
test_gaps.property: An empty or whitespace-only log_level — from ANY layer that wins the precedence merge — resolves to "info" rather than passing an empty string through to main's EnvFilter::try_new(""), which builds an empty filter that silently disables ALL log output. Crucially, an empty value in a higher-precedence layer shadows a non-empty lower layer and STILL falls back to "info" (it does not fall through to the lower layer's value).
test_gaps.test_design:
Three sub-cases in one #[cfg(test)] mod test added to config/mod.rs, using the existing tmp_dir helper.
(1) Direct fallback: file layer log_level = Some("") → assert resolved c.log_level == "info".
(2) Whitespace-only: file layer log_level = Some("   ") → assert c.log_level == "info" (proves the .trim() in the filter predicate, not just is_empty()).
(3) Precedence interaction (the load-bearing subtlety): env layer log_level = Some("") AND file layer log_level = Some("debug"). Because merge is cli.or(env).or(file), the empty env value WINS the merge, then the F58 .filter drops it → assert c.log_level == "info" (NOT "debug"). This pins that an empty higher layer does not silently inherit a lower layer's value, and never leaks an empty string downstream.
Also assert a normal non-empty value still passes through: file layer log_level = Some("debug") with no empty higher layer → c.log_level == "debug" (already covered by file_layer_overrides_defaults, so optional; include only if keeping the proposal self-contained).
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: Read all 28 from_layers tests + the parse_bool/redacted-url tests in config/mod.rs (lines 886-1536): defaults_applied_for_non_path_fields only asserts the default "info" when log_level is unset (None); file_layer_overrides_defaults asserts a normal "debug" passes through. NO test sets log_level to an empty/whitespace string, so the F58 filter predicate `.filter(|s| !s.trim().is_empty())` (mod.rs:666-668) is entirely unexercised, and the precedence-shadowing case is untested. grep across crates/ for log_level/F58/EnvFilter+test/empty/trim returns only testkit harness builders and a system.rs that hard-codes log_level="error" — none assert the empty-string fallback. The source comment (mod.rs:663-665) explicitly flags this as load-bearing: an empty value would make EnvFilter::try_new("") disable all logging silently.
test_gaps.verify_reason: F58 filter (mod.rs:666-668) is genuinely untested — no existing config test sets empty/whitespace log_level; the bug premise is verified real (try_new("") returns Ok with zero directives → silent log disable, NOT rescued by main's unwrap_or_else), the precedence-shadowing case is load-bearing, and I confirmed the test compiles+passes deterministically.

test_gaps.title: End-to-end no-loss-across-crash through the drain: an acked record survives SIGKILL+restart and is actually delivered to the sink and the recovered segment confirmed (not merely re-queued)
test_gaps.area: Durability invariant (c) end-to-end — crash → restart → recovery → replay → drain → sink → confirm, across WAB+drain modules
test_gaps.target: Integration: crates/weir-server/tests/system.rs (crash-recovery section) — the full path main.rs::replay_unconfirmed → drain::process_segment → confirm_and_delete after a SIGKILL+restart
test_gaps.property: After an acked Sync record is durably written and the daemon is SIGKILL'd before graceful shutdown, a restart not only RE-QUEUES the recovered segment (which existing tests prove via recovery_records_replayed_total) but the drain actually delivers the recovered records to the sink AND confirms+deletes the segment — i.e. weir_sink_commit_records_total{outcome="committed"} advances by >= the acked count post-restart, and the recovered sealed segment ends up confirmed (no orphaned undrained .wab.sealed left behind). This closes the gap between 'segment queued to the drain' and 'records delivered + segment confirmed'.
test_gaps.test_design: Mirror `recovery_replays_records_after_crash` but extend the assertions past the replay-queue metric to the delivery+confirm end state. Use `weir_server!("crash_to_sink").shard_count(1).start()` (default NoopSink, which always commits Ok — a known-good sink so any non-delivery is a real bug, not a sink failure). Push N=25 Sync records (all acked durable). `srv.kill_ungracefully()` then `srv.restart_in_place()`. Poll metrics (loop up to ~3s, sleeping 50ms) until `weir_sink_commit_records_total{outcome="committed"}` >= N OR timeout. Assert: (1) committed >= N — recovered records were actually delivered to the sink on this post-restart session (counter reset to 0 at restart, so this is purely post-restart delivery); (2) after delivery settles, count `.wab.sealed` files under srv.wab_dir == 0 (every recovered segment was confirmed+deleted, none stranded) using the existing `count_wab_files(&srv.wab_dir, ".wab.sealed")` helper; (3) sanity that recovery_records_replayed_total >= N (the queue step also happened). Distinct from existing tests: `recovery_replays_records_after_crash` stops at recovery_records_replayed (bumped at replay-QUEUE time in mod.rs:386, before any sink contact); `wab_data_preserved_across_crash_restart` checks bytes + the same replay metric; the mysql/postgres/clickhouse sink tests assert committed counts but are #[ignore]d (need a live DB) and never crash+restart. No CI-runnable test proves the recovered-segment → sink → confirm leg end to end.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: Read both crash-recovery system tests fully. `recovery_replays_records_after_crash` (system.rs:1856) and `wab_data_preserved_across_crash_restart` (system.rs:749) BOTH stop their assertion at `weir_recovery_records_replayed_total`, which is incremented in replay_unconfirmed at mod.rs:386 the moment a segment PATH is sent to the drain channel — strictly before the drain opens it, reads it, calls sink.commit, or writes .confirmed. So both tests pass even if the post-restart drain never delivers a single recovered record to the sink (e.g. a regression that wedges the drain after replay, or confirms-without-committing). The sink-delivery counter `weir_sink_commit_records_total{outcome=committed}` is asserted only by mysql_sink_end_to_end / postgres / clickhouse tests (system.rs:1917+) which are all #[ignore]d (require a live DB) and none of them crash+restart. The DST harness drives the flusher write/recovery side and explicitly never runs the drain. No CI-runnable test ties together restart → replay → drain → NoopSink delivery → segment confirmed, so the second half of the no-loss-across-crash chain (the part that actually gets acked data to its destination) is unproven end to end.
test_gaps.verify_reason: Verified gap is real: both CI crash tests stop at recovery_records_replayed (bumped at replay-QUEUE time, wab/mod.rs:386, before sink contact); committed-metric assertions exist only in #[ignore]d DB-backed sink tests that never crash-restart, and load.rs never crashes — so restart→replay→drain→NoopSink→confirm is unproven in CI. NoopSink commits the whole batch and confirm_and_delete deletes the .wab.sealed, so the property is load-bearing. Assertion (1) is deterministic (fresh post-restart process, metrics reset to 0, generous poll). Caveat: assertion (2) has a sub-ms race — committed>=N is bumped (drain/mod.rs:942) before confirm_and_delete runs (drain/mod.rs:537), and the draft counts .wab.sealed immediately with no poll, so it should poll for leftover_sealed==0; a fixable hardening, not a flaw in the property.

test_gaps.title: SegmentReader::next pre-allocation cap on a corrupt on-disk record length is untested
test_gaps.area: robustness/security — disk-read DoS bound + no-false-ack on injected corruption
test_gaps.property: A sealed segment whose per-record length field declares more than MAX_PAYLOAD_HARD_CAP bytes (forged/corrupt/bit-rot) is rejected with an InvalidData Err BEFORE any heap allocation, and the reader marks itself done so it yields no further records. The drain consumes this Err as read_failed and quarantines the segment (never confirm+delete), so the no-false-ack/no-data-loss invariant holds. A regression that dropped or weakened this check would attempt a ~4 GiB allocation during a hot drain read (recovery OOM / DoS) or, worse, mis-handle the Err and let the drain confirm+delete an unread segment.
test_gaps.test_design: Setup: create a WAB segment via WabSegment::create(&path, 0), write one valid record (b"keep-me"), drop the segment to flush. Then splice a raw oversized 4-byte length field over the position where the next record's length prefix would begin (append (MAX_PAYLOAD_HARD_CAP as u32 + 1).to_le_bytes() to the active file — the same append_raw technique recovery.rs::recovery_truncates_at_oversized_payload_len uses, except here we drive SegmentReader directly rather than recover_segment). SegmentReader::open only validates magic+version, so it opens the active .wab file fine. Assertions: (1) reader.next() yields Some(Ok(record)) equal to b"keep-me"; (2) the NEXT reader.next() yields Some(Err(e)) with e.kind()==ErrorKind::InvalidData and e.to_string() containing "exceeds MAX_PAYLOAD_HARD_CAP"; (3) a subsequent reader.next() yields None (the reader set done=true on the error and does not loop/re-read). This pins the SegmentReader cap branch, which is DISTINCT from the covered paths: recovery.rs::recovery_truncates_at_oversized_payload_len (F17) tests recover_segment's separate truncate-and-seal-prefix branch, and segment_reader_detects_crc_mismatch (mod.rs:1037) tests the CRC branch — neither feeds SegmentReader an over-cap length. The cap-check is the disk-side twin of the (well-tested) connection.rs and envelope.rs cap checks, but on the drain's delivery read path; it is the only allocation-bounding guard there and has zero coverage.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: Checked every SegmentReader test in crates/weir-server/src/wab/mod.rs::tests (segment_reader_round_trip:910, segment_reader_detects_crc_mismatch:1037, read_segment_record_count_rejects_short_file_clearly:929) and every SegmentReader::open call across recovery.rs (lines 502/527/553/598/684/721/753), drain/mod.rs (643/1955/2356/2422), and dst.rs (720): none feeds SegmentReader a record whose length field exceeds MAX_PAYLOAD_HARD_CAP. The only oversized-len coverage, recovery.rs::recovery_truncates_at_oversized_payload_len (F17, line 707), exercises recover_segment's separate code path (recovery.rs:255-261, which TRUNCATES and seals the clean prefix) — fundamentally different behavior from SegmentReader::next (mod.rs:857-865, which returns Err and sets done=true). The night sweep's S33 covered recovery's CRC-truncate branch, not this. proptest_envelope.rs and the wab_confirmed/envelope_parse fuzz targets do not touch SegmentReader. The branch is reached by the drain (drain/mod.rs:643/677-689) on every sealed-segment read, so it guards the live delivery path, not just startup recovery.
test_gaps.verify_reason: Verified by running the exact draft: the SegmentReader::next over-cap branch (mod.rs:857-865) has zero coverage — recovery's F17 test hits the distinct recover_segment truncate branch, not this Err+done path — and the test is meaningful (guards the drain's live no-OOM/no-false-ack invariant) and deterministic.

test_gaps.title: read_body_capped — the sink response-body memory bound (S28) — has no test exercising the cap
test_gaps.area: robustness/security — network-read size cap against a hostile/compromised downstream
test_gaps.target: crates/weir-server/src/sink/mod.rs::read_body_capped (the cap-enforcement branch: take < chunk.len() => break, lines 95-108)
test_gaps.property: read_body_capped buffers at most `cap` bytes regardless of how large a body the downstream sends (including a body far larger than the cap, or one that exceeds the cap inside a single chunk), then stops and returns. This bounds peak memory at O(concurrency × cap) so a hostile or compromised sink endpoint cannot make the daemon buffer an unbounded response into memory (the S28 fix). A regression to resp.bytes()/resp.text() or an off-by-one in the take/break logic would silently reintroduce the unbounded-allocation DoS.
test_gaps.test_design:
This needs a reqwest::Response over a real (loopback) connection because read_body_capped takes reqwest::Response and drives resp.chunk(). Reuse the http.rs test scaffolding pattern (spawn a tokio TcpListener mock on 127.0.0.1:0). Mock server: accept one connection, read the request header block (find_double_crlf, as the existing http.rs mocks do), then write a response whose body is much larger than the cap — e.g. 'HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Length: 1048576

' followed by 1 MiB of 0x41 bytes. Client side: build a reqwest::Client, GET/POST the URL to obtain a reqwest::Response, then call read_body_capped(resp, 64) with a deliberately tiny cap. Assertions: (1) the returned Vec length is EXACTLY 64 (cap), never the full 1 MiB — proves the cap is enforced and the read stops, including the mid-chunk break since 64 < first chunk; (2) every returned byte is 0x41 (we actually read body bytes, not header/garbage); (3) a second call with cap = RESPONSE_BODY_CAP (64 KiB) over the same oversized-body server returns exactly 64*1024 bytes, pinning the production constant against an unbounded read. Place the test under #[cfg(any(feature="http-sink",feature="clickhouse-sink"))] in sink/mod.rs::tests since read_body_capped is gated to those features. Optionally also assert the call returns promptly (wrap in tokio::time::timeout) so a regression that drains the whole body can't pass by being merely slow.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: grep for read_body_capped / RESPONSE_BODY_CAP across crates/weir-server shows the function is referenced only at its definition (sink/mod.rs:93) and its two call sites (http.rs:171,222; clickhouse.rs:382) — no test names it. Read all of http.rs::tests and clickhouse.rs::tests: every mock server (spawn_mock_server, spawn_header_capture_server, the inline mocks) sends 'Content-Length: 0' or bodies of a handful of bytes ('bad payload!', 'no!'), so the cap branch (take < chunk.len() => break, mod.rs:101-102) and the cap loop bound are never exercised — the existing http_400_dead_letters / clickhouse 4xx tests only assert the 256/500-char EXCERPT length, downstream of the cap, never the cap itself. The night sweep landed S03/S14/S30-S34 as tests; S28 (this body cap) is a separate finding whose proposed test was NOT in the landed set, and the sweep doc (line 241) explicitly notes the missing 'streaming/cap unit test asserting the read stops after N bytes'. So the S28 fix shipped with no regression guard.
test_gaps.verify_reason: Confirmed: read_body_capped / RESPONSE_BODY_CAP (mod.rs:86,93-109) are named only at the def + 3 call sites; every existing mock sends Content-Length:0 or <=12-byte bodies, so the cap loop and mid-chunk break (mod.rs:100-102) are never exercised — the S28 fix shipped with no regression guard. The proposed test is meaningful (exact-length + real-bytes assertions catch a resp.bytes() regression and off-by-one) and deterministic (final len assertions hold for any TCP chunking since the loop runs until buf.len()>=cap; 5s loopback timeout is safe).

test_gaps.title: S42 fix is wired but untested at the field level: a relative or '..' TLS cert/key/CA path is not proven to be rejected through config validation
test_gaps.area: config / TCP+TLS path validation (crates/weir-server/src/config/mod.rs::from_layers, require_tls_path! macro)
test_gaps.property: When tcp_bind is set, a tls_*_path that is relative, contains '..', or contains a null byte is rejected by from_layers with a PathInvalid error naming that field — i.e. TLS paths get the same format hardening as socket_path/wab_dir (S42), not merely an existence check.
test_gaps.test_design: S42 added `validate_path_format($fname, path)?;` inside the require_tls_path! macro (config/mod.rs:707) so TLS paths stop bypassing the absolute/no-'..'/no-null format checks. EXISTING coverage: validate_path_format_rejects_relative / validate_path_format_accepts_nonexistent_absolute test the helper DIRECTLY; tcp_bind_without_tls_paths_is_rejected tests a MISSING path; valid_client_cert_pushes_succeed etc. test the runtime handshake with absolute temp paths. NONE feeds a relative or '..'-containing TLS path through from_layers and asserts rejection — so the S42 WIRING (the macro line calling the helper) could be deleted and every test would still pass while the security regression (a '..' traversal TLS path silently accepted up to the existence check) returns. SETUP (gated #[cfg(feature = "tls")], in config/mod.rs tests): build a PartialConfig with tcp_bind = Some("127.0.0.1:7100"), wab_dir = an existing temp dir, and tls_cert_path = Some(PathBuf::from("relative/cert.pem")) (and a second case with PathBuf::from("/etc/weir/../../etc/shadow")). ASSERTIONS: from_layers returns Err; the error is ConfigError::PathInvalid; err.to_string() contains "tls_cert_path" AND mentions "absolute" (relative case) or "'..'" (dotdot case). Repeat for tls_key_path and tls_client_ca_path to pin all three field names. This proves the wiring, complementing the direct helper tests.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: Grep over config/mod.rs for 'tls_cert.*\.\.', 'tls.*relative', 'validate_path_format.*tls' returns nothing. The TLS-related config tests are: tcp_bind_invalid_addr_is_rejected, no_tcp_bind_defaults_to_unix_only, tcp_bind_without_tls_paths_is_rejected (missing paths), tcp_bind_without_tls_feature_is_rejected. validate_path_format_rejects_relative / _accepts_nonexistent_absolute exercise the HELPER but not via a tls_*_path field. So the S42 line (mod.rs:707 `validate_path_format($fname, path)?;`) is the only thing standing between an attacker-supplied '..'/relative TLS path and the existence check, and no test would fail if it were removed.
test_gaps.verify_reason: Verified at mod.rs:707: the S42 line validate_path_format($fname,path)? inside require_tls_path! is untested through from_layers — existing tests hit the helper directly (socket_path/test fields) or test MISSING paths (InvalidValue, not PathInvalid); deleting line 707 fails no test yet re-opens traversal/relative TLS cert/key/CA paths. Test is meaningful (pins all 3 fields + PathInvalid variant + 'absolute'/'..' messages matching mod.rs:820,826) and deterministic (pure sync config validation, absolute '..' case correctly reaches the dotdot check).

test_gaps.title: A resumed segment (skip>0) that hits a read error inside its already-processed prefix must quarantine, not panic or confirm
test_gaps.area: Drain subsystem — process_segment resume (F05) + quarantine interaction (G10)
test_gaps.target: crates/weir-server/src/drain/mod.rs::process_segment — the read_failed early-return at lines 743-748, reached with skip > 0 (durable_through == skip < read_index), guarding the debug_assert_eq!(durable_through, read_index) at line 801
test_gaps.property: When process_segment is invoked with skip = N > 0 (a resumed retry per F05) and the segment now fails to read a record (e.g. on-disk corruption that appeared between attempts), the function must take the read_failed path: deliver any readable post-skip prefix, quarantine the segment, and return ProcessResult::Quarantined — WITHOUT tripping the durable_through == read_index debug assertion (in debug builds the assert would panic; the comment at L796-800 says this read_failed-before-assert ordering is deliberate precisely so a resumed-into-corruption segment is a legitimate, non-panicking state). It must never confirm+delete the segment with an unread tail.
test_gaps.test_design: SETUP: make_sealed_segment(&dir, 0, &[b"r1", b"r2", b"r3"]). Corrupt record 2's payload bytes on disk (same technique as mid_segment_read_error_quarantines_not_deletes at L1756 — flip the byte at offset 42, the start of record 2's payload region) so the reader yields r1 then errors at r2. Call block_on(process_segment(&sealed, &sink, &config, &metrics, &mut dl, /*skip=*/1)) — resume as if r1 was already durably processed on a prior attempt. ASSERTIONS: (1) result matches ProcessResult::Quarantined (the read error wins regardless of skip; reached via the read_failed early-return, NOT via confirm). (2) !sealed.exists() and the quarantine dir contains exactly one file (segment moved, not deleted, not confirmed). (3) records_dead_lettered(&metrics) == 0 (the skipped r1 is NOT re-dead-lettered; the read-error tail is quarantined, not dead-lettered). (4) records_committed(&metrics) == 0 (r1 was skipped; nothing readable after the skip). Run under the normal debug test build so that, if a future refactor moved the read_failed return below the debug_assert, the panic surfaces. This is the F05+quarantine interaction the existing tests never combine: the clean-resume test uses no corruption, and both quarantine tests use skip=0.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: F05 is covered only by retry_resumes_past_already_processed_sub_batches (L1483), which is a CLEAN resume (no corruption on the second attempt). The two read-error/quarantine tests — mid_segment_read_error_quarantines_not_deletes (L1756) and post_seal_tail_truncation_quarantines_not_confirms (L1798) — both call process_segment with skip=0, so neither exercises the skip>0 + read_failed combination. The code comment at mod.rs:796-800 explicitly documents that the debug_assert at L801 is positioned AFTER the read_failed return precisely because a resumed segment with a mid-prefix read error is a legitimate state (durable_through == skip < read_index) that would otherwise trip the assert — yet no test drives that interaction. The night sweep (S01-S46) added S01 (tail-truncation, skip=0) but nothing for resume+corruption.
test_gaps.verify_reason: Genuinely uncovered, meaningful, deterministic: no existing test combines skip>0 with a read error (F05 resume test L1483 is clean; both quarantine tests L1756/L1798 use skip=0). Traced skip=1 + byte-42 corruption: r1 skipped (read_index=1, continue), r2 errors (break), batch empty so commit_batch never runs — read_failed wins, returns Quarantined, dead_lettered=0, committed=0; segment moved to wab_dir/quarantine. All four assertions hold; fully deterministic (no timing/ordering). Caveat: the proposal over-claims the debug_assert angle — at the read_failed return durable_through(1)==read_index(1), so the assert would actually PASS here and the test would NOT catch a refactor moving the assert above the read_failed check. But the core property (resume + mid-prefix read error must quarantine, not confirm or re-dead-letter the skipped prefix) is real and uncovered, so the test is worthwhile.

test_gaps.title: HTTP sink permanent 4xx body excerpt is not truncated/bounded by any test
test_gaps.target: crates/weir-server/src/sink/http.rs::HttpSink::post_record — permanent branch at http.rs:215-229 (`let cut = bytes.len().min(256)`)
test_gaps.property: When a downstream returns a permanent (4xx, non-408/429) status with a large response body, the dead-letter reason string carries only a bounded excerpt (<= 256 bytes of body text), never the full body — so a hostile/verbose endpoint cannot inflate the dead-letter reason / log line without bound.
test_gaps.test_design: Setup: use the existing spawn_mock_server scaffolding but return a 400 with a large body, e.g. a Content-Length-prefixed response whose body is 4096 'A' bytes (`let big = "A".repeat(4096)`; build the response string with `Content-Length: 4096`). Drive `sink.commit(vec![p(b"x")])` and unwrap the Ok CommitResult. Assertions: (1) result.committed is empty and result.dead_lettered.len()==1; (2) the reason string contains "400"; (3) the body portion is bounded — assert the reason length is small relative to the body: the reason is `format!("http {status}: {body_excerpt}")` and body_excerpt is at most 256 chars, so assert `result.dead_lettered[0].1.len() < 400` (covers the "http 400 Bad Request: " prefix + <=256 excerpt) and that it does NOT contain the full 4096-char run (e.g. `!reason.contains(&"A".repeat(300))`). Note the read cap is 64KiB and the excerpt cut is min(256), so a 4KiB body fully passes the read cap and the only bound that fires is the 256-byte excerpt — exactly the line under test. Mock-server note: spawn_mock_server reads the request body by Content-Length then writes the canned response; the existing http_400_dead_letters test confirms this path works with a short body.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: Checked every #[tokio::test] in http.rs's test module (http.rs:428-1185). The only permanent-4xx tests — http_400_dead_letters (http.rs:684, 12-byte body 'bad payload!') and http_401_dead_letters (http.rs:757, empty body) — use bodies far under 256 bytes, so the `bytes.len().min(256)` cut at http.rs:223 never engages. No test in the module uses `.repeat(` or sends a body larger than a few bytes. The body-cap reasoning is documented (S28/S29 at http.rs:215-225) but the truncation boundary itself is unverified; the night sweep (S30/S31) covered only credential redaction, not the excerpt bound.
test_gaps.verify_reason: The 256-byte excerpt cut at http.rs:223 (`bytes.len().min(256)`) is genuinely unexercised — all existing permanent-4xx tests (http_400 12-byte body, http_401/mixed-batch empty bodies) stay far under 256B, and the sanitize_log_excerpt unit test only checks control-char stripping, not the length bound; the test is meaningful (bounds documented S28/S29 hostile-downstream output in dead-letter reasons) and deterministic (single ASCII record, single canned response, no timing/ordering races, cut at byte 256 of single-byte 'A' is valid UTF-8 so the assertions hold exactly).

test_gaps.title: ClickHouse percent_decode multibyte-UTF-8 decode (and lossy fallback) is untested
test_gaps.property: percent_decode reassembles multi-byte UTF-8 from consecutive %XX escapes correctly (e.g. `%C3%A9` -> 'é'), so a credential with non-ASCII characters authenticates as the intended Unicode string; and an escape sequence that decodes to invalid UTF-8 is handled losslessly (no panic, replacement char) rather than crashing the build/commit path.
test_gaps.test_design: Pure-function unit test in clickhouse.rs::tests calling percent_decode directly (no network). Cases: (1) `percent_decode("caf%C3%A9")` == "café" — proves consecutive escapes are accumulated into the byte buffer BEFORE the from_utf8 conversion (a naive per-escape `char::from(byte)` impl would produce two Latin-1 chars 'Ã©' instead); (2) `percent_decode("%E2%82%AC")` == "€" (3-byte sequence); (3) mixed literal+escape `percent_decode("a%C3%A9b")` == "aéb"; (4) invalid-UTF-8 escape `percent_decode("%FF")` must NOT panic and must go through from_utf8_lossy — assert it returns the replacement char `"\u{FFFD}"` (or at minimum `assert!(!percent_decode("%FF").is_empty())` to pin the no-panic + lossy contract). This also indirectly pins the credential path: split_credentials feeds percent_decode, so a multibyte password is decoded correctly before basic_auth.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: clickhouse.rs::tests has exactly one direct percent_decode test — percent_decode_leaves_invalid_escapes_verbatim (clickhouse.rs:614) — which only covers truncated (`ab%2`), non-hex (`a%zzb`), and plain inputs. The credential tests split_credentials_percent_decodes_userinfo (clickhouse.rs:605) uses `p%40ss%21` which decodes to ASCII `p@ss!` only. Grep for `%C3`/`%E2`/`multibyte`/`utf` in the file finds none touching percent_decode; from_utf8_lossy (clickhouse.rs:276) is therefore exercised only on already-valid-ASCII bytes, and the multi-escape accumulation + lossy-invalid-UTF-8 branches are unproven. F34 is the documented driver for this code.
test_gaps.verify_reason: Verified against clickhouse.rs:257-277: no existing test reaches the multibyte byte-accumulation or invalid-UTF-8 lossy branch (existing tests use ASCII-only decode + verbatim-leave inputs); all four asserted outputs trace correctly through the actual impl and the test is a deterministic pure function.

test_gaps.title: Invalid metrics_bind IP string is rejected with a field-named error, not parsed into a surprising address
test_gaps.property: A metrics_bind value that is not a parseable IpAddr is rejected at config load with a ConfigError naming the metrics_bind field, and a valid address (e.g. "0.0.0.0") parses through to the resolved IpAddr. The error path (the .map_err on parse::<IpAddr>()) must fire — a malformed bind address must not be silently coerced or default-substituted.
test_gaps.test_design:
Two sub-cases using the existing tmp_dir + PartialConfig scaffolding.
(1) Reject: set file layer metrics_bind = Some("not-an-ip") → from_layers returns Err; assert err.to_string() contains "metrics_bind". Also try a host:port form like "127.0.0.1:9185" (a common operator mistake — metrics_bind takes only the IP, the port is metrics_port) to prove that too is rejected.
(2) Accept + parse: set metrics_bind = Some("0.0.0.0") → from_layers Ok; assert c.metrics_bind == IpAddr::V4(Ipv4Addr::UNSPECIFIED). Also assert an unset metrics_bind defaults to 127.0.0.1 (the localhost-only security default) — pins that the default branch and the parse branch agree.
Import std::net::{IpAddr, Ipv4Addr} in the test (IpAddr is already in scope at module level via super::*).
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: Read the full config/mod.rs test module (28 from_layers tests). The TCP-address parse error IS tested (tcp_bind_invalid_addr_is_rejected, mod.rs:1390), but the analogous metrics_bind IpAddr parse error path (mod.rs:509-518) has NO test, and NO test asserts the resolved metrics_bind value (including the localhost-only default at mod.rs:508). grep for metrics_bind across crates/ returns only doc comments (mod.rs:505, main.rs:455) and the testkit harness field — no assertion on the parse-success or parse-failure branch. The default 127.0.0.1 is a documented security choice (Config::metrics_bind docs warn against exposing counters to the LAN), so a regression that mis-parses or mis-defaults the bind address is a real operability/exposure hazard.
test_gaps.verify_reason: Verified: metrics_bind IpAddr parse error path (mod.rs:511-518) and the localhost-only default (mod.rs:508) have no test — grep shows only doc/plumbing refs, and only the analogous tcp_bind path is tested. Test is meaningful (pins a documented security default + error branch), deterministic (pure sync config parse), and the draft compiles against real helpers/imports.

test_gaps.title: Env-var config layer has zero tests — the F57 lenient-bool wiring and int-parse error path are unproven
test_gaps.property: Boolean env vars accept the lenient spellings (1/0, yes/no, on/off, TRUE) via parse_bool — NOT bool::from_str — and a non-numeric integer env var surfaces a ConfigError::ParseError naming the field, rather than panicking or being silently dropped.
test_gaps.test_design: env.rs has NO #[cfg(test)] module at all (confirmed: grep for 'mod tests' in env.rs is empty; the file is 97 LOC of pure parsing logic). The only related coverage is config/mod.rs::parse_bool_truthy_values / parse_bool_falsey_values / parse_bool_rejects_garbage — but those call parse_bool DIRECTLY; they do not exercise env::env_bool, which is the layer that decides WHETHER env bools go through parse_bool (F57) vs the old env_parse::<bool> that rejected everything but exact 'true'/'false'. A regression that changed line 78 `super::parse_bool(key, &v).map(Some)` back to `v.parse::<bool>()` would re-break WEIR_PEER_UID_CHECK=1 / WEIR_SINK_SEND_IDEMPOTENCY_KEY=on and no current test would fail. SETUP: env.rs functions are private (pub(super)); test inside a new #[cfg(test)] mod in env.rs (or via super::). Because env vars are process-global, the test must set+remove a uniquely-named var and run serially — gate the std::env::set_var calls in one #[test] fn and clean up with remove_var, OR (preferred, avoids global-env flakiness) refactor-free approach: add the test asserting env_bool's behavior by setting a one-off var. ASSERTIONS: (1) set WEIR_PEER_UID_CHECK='1' -> env_bool returns Ok(Some(true)); set 'off' -> Ok(Some(false)); set 'TRUE' -> Ok(Some(true)); (2) unset var -> Ok(None); (3) env_parse::<u32> on a var set to 'not-a-number' returns Err(ConfigError::ParseError { field, .. }) whose field == the var name; (4) env_parse on an unset var -> Ok(None). NOTE on weakness in adjacent coverage: the config layer-merge tests (env_layer_overrides_file) set fields via PartialConfig structs directly, bypassing env::read entirely, so the env READING path (var->parse->ConfigError) is wholly untested end to end.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: env.rs has no #[cfg(test)] module (grep 'mod tests' / '#[cfg(test)]' over env.rs returns nothing; file lists in the 'ZERO in-file test module' set). Searched all of crates/weir-server for any test invoking env::read, env_bool, env_parse, or std::env::set_var("WEIR_...") — only load.rs/system.rs reference WEIR_* env vars and those are benchmark/integration knobs, not the env-layer parse logic. config/mod.rs tests cover parse_bool directly and cover the merge precedence via PartialConfig structs, but never drive a real env var through env::read into a ConfigError. The F57 fix (env_bool -> parse_bool instead of env_parse::<bool>) is therefore wired but unproven.
test_gaps.verify_reason: Verified: env.rs has zero #[cfg(test)] (count=0); env_bool (line 78) routes through super::parse_bool not bool::from_str, and env_parse wraps failures in ConfigError::ParseError{field:key,..} — both reading paths are exercised by NO existing test (mod.rs tests call parse_bool directly and merge-test via PartialConfig, never env::read), so the F57 wiring is a genuine, meaningful, currently-unproven gap. Minor proposal error (env_bool/env_parse are plain `fn`, not pub(super)) doesn't affect the design since super::* reaches private items. Determinism caveat: std::env is process-global, but no other test in the crate mutates these vars, so it's safe today if run serially.

test_gaps.title: queue::try_push (the connection handler's lock-free fast path) has no direct test for its Err-on-full / Err-on-disconnected contract
test_gaps.property: try_push returns Ok(()) when the chosen partition has capacity, and returns Err(unit) — handing the work unit BACK so the caller can fall through to push_timeout — when the partition is full or every receiver has been dropped. The returned unit on Err must be the same value that was passed in (no loss/duplication).
test_gaps.test_design: connection.rs:368 makes try_push the production fast path: Ok => proceed, Err(unit) => spawn_blocking(push_timeout). The Err arm's correctness (unit handed back, exactly once) is the precondition for the no-false-ack backpressure behaviour. queue.rs tests cover push (test-only blocking), push_timeout (full-timeout, slot-available, disconnected), and partition routing — but try_push is never called from any test (grep: try_push appears only in its definition and in connection.rs). SETUP: use new_with_capacity::<u32>(1, 1) so the single partition holds exactly one slot. ASSERTIONS: (1) first try_push(0, 1) returns Ok(()); (2) second try_push(0, 2) on the now-full partition returns Err(2) — the exact unit back, not a different/duplicated value; (3) after the receiver drains item 1, try_push(0, 3) returns Ok(()) again (capacity restored); (4) separately, drop the receiver then try_push returns Err with the unit back (Disconnected mapped to Err, matching the macro arm `Full(u) | Disconnected(u) => Err(u)`). This pins both Err sub-cases the single match arm collapses together.
test_gaps.uncovered_evidence: grep 'try_push' across crates/weir-server: only the definition (queue.rs:90) and the single production caller (connection.rs:368). No test references it. queue.rs's existing tests cover push (test-only) and push_timeout's Timeout/slot-available/Disconnected arms, but try_push's TrySendError::Full / ::Disconnected -> Err(u) arm (queue.rs:95) — the path that feeds the connection handler's spawn_blocking fallback and ultimately the InternalError nack — is entirely unexercised.
test_gaps.verify_reason: try_push is the production fast path (socket/connection.rs:368) yet has zero test coverage — grep confirms it appears only in its definition (queue.rs:90) and that one caller; the Full|Disconnected => Err(u) arm (line 95) feeding the spawn_blocking/push_timeout fallback and InternalError nack is entirely unexercised. The test is meaningful (with T=u32, Err(2)/Err(99) assert the unit is handed back unchanged, the precondition for no-false-ack backpressure) and deterministic (fully synchronous, no timing/ordering races).

logic_problems.title: Short-header quarantine path silently skips the quarantine metrics that every other quarantine branch increments
logic_problems.property: A segment file shorter than SEGMENT_HEADER_LEN (24 bytes) is quarantined, an Err is returned, AND the quarantine is observable via metrics — specifically `recovery_segments_quarantined` and `wab_segments{state=quarantined}` — consistently with the bad-magic and bad-version branches.
logic_problems.test_design: SUSPECTED LOGIC PROBLEM (do not lock in current behavior). Setup: tmp dir; write a `.wab` file of fewer than 24 bytes (e.g. `seg_00000001.wab` containing just `b"WEI"`, 3 bytes). Call `recover_segment(&path, &dir, &metrics)`. Observed/expected divergence: the bad-magic branch (recovery.rs:184-191) and bad-version branch (203-210) both call `metrics.recovery_segments_quarantined.inc()` AND bump `wab_segments{state=quarantined}` after quarantining. The header-too-short branch (165-174) quarantines via `quarantine(...)` and returns Err but does NEITHER. So a corrupt segment whose header was torn off (a plausible crash/at-rest-truncation shape — partial first block) is quarantined invisibly: operators alerting on `weir_recovery_segments_quarantined` never see it, and the cumulative `weir_wab_segments` state-transition count is wrong. ASSERTIONS the corrected code should satisfy: (1) `result.is_err()` with kind InvalidData and message containing "shorter than segment header"; (2) `!path.exists()` (moved out); (3) `dir.join("quarantine")` contains the file; (4) `metrics.recovery_segments_quarantined.get() == 1`; (5) `wab_segments.get_or_create(&SegmentStateLabel{state: SegmentState::quarantined}).get() == 1`. Because assertions (4) and (5) FAIL against today's code, flag for a human: either the metric calls belong in the short-header branch too, or all three quarantine branches should funnel through a single helper that always increments. Do not assert the current (zero-metric) behavior as correct.
logic_problems.uncovered_evidence: recovery.rs tests cover bad-magic (recovery_quarantines_bad_magic, asserts recovery_segments_quarantined==1), and the .confirmed quarantine paths (check_confirmed_bad_crc_quarantines, check_confirmed_unknown_version_quarantines_with_specific_message). NO test exercises the header-too-short branch at recovery.rs:165-174. Grep for "shorter than" / "short.*header" across crates/weir-server/src finds only the production strings and a footer-length test in mod.rs (read_segment_record_count_rejects_short_file_clearly — a different function). The metric-omission in this branch is therefore both untested and, on inspection, inconsistent with the two sibling quarantine branches that DO increment both counters.
logic_problems.verify_reason: Verified: short-header branch (recovery.rs:165-174) quarantines + returns Err but omits the recovery_segments_quarantined.inc() and wab_segments{quarantined} bumps that bad-magic (185-191) and bad-version (204-210) both do; no existing test covers this branch — a real, plausible observability inconsistency worth a human flag.
